source.title: Experiencing oneself vs another person as being the cause of an action: the neural correlates of the experience of agency

confidence_rationale: Farrer & Frith provide a clean, direct fMRI dissociation: anterior insula activates when subjects experience themselves as the cause of an action; inferior parietal cortex activates when they experience another person as the cause. This double dissociation is exactly what MECH-095 predicts -- the comparator region (here: inferior parietal / TPJ) activates specifically when self-attribution fails and external attribution is triggered. The paradigm is tightly controlled (same motor task and same visual stimuli across conditions; only the attribution changes), making this strong causal evidence rather than correlational.
failure_signatures: If TPJ/inferior parietal were not the comparator but instead a general salience region, it would activate for both self- and other-caused conditions -- the study finds the opposite.

If EXQ-121 AgencyComparator failure (AUC_COMP_ON < AUC_COMP_ABLATED) reflects a scale/training problem rather than a conceptual error, this paper's evidence for the underlying neural mechanism remains intact.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Self-agency (knowing you caused an action) is associated with anterior insula activation. Attribution of an action to another person is associated with inferior parietal cortex (right IPL/TPJ area) activation. The inferior parietal cortex operates in an allocentric coding system applicable to others' as well as one's own actions.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-095 proposes that the TPJ-equivalent comparator detects divergence between efference-copy-predicted z_self change and observed z_self change, triggering world-attribution (residue candidate) when they diverge. Farrer & Frith's finding that the inferior parietal / TPJ activates precisely when external attribution occurs -- not when self-attribution succeeds -- is consistent with the comparator firing a mismatch signal. In REE terms: AgencyComparator.output is not a continuous self-confidence score but rather a binary or graded mismatch flag; when it fires, the event is routed to z_world residue rather than suppressed.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper tests phenomenological attribution under voluntary instruction ('believe the experimenter is driving this'), not an automatic comparator between motor prediction and sensory outcome. The allocentric/egocentric framing is richer than a simple efference-copy comparator. REE's AgencyComparator must eventually handle the difference between voluntary causal belief and automatic motor prediction error.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI with right-handed male subjects performing a joystick task to drive a cursor along a T-shaped path. Attribution (self vs. other) was manipulated by verbal instruction while motor demands were identical. PET activation results confirmed with SPM.

confidence_components.notes: This is a seminal, highly cited paper with a clean experimental design; the double dissociation is robust. Mapping fidelity is good but not perfect because the task manipulates belief-level attribution rather than sensory prediction error per se. Transfer risk is moderate: human voluntary attribution task maps to REE's automated latent-space comparator only if the underlying neural comparator is the same mechanism operating at different timescales.
